memes.label: Alexander Pope
memes.description: English poet (1688–1744) whose Essay on Man articulates a philosophical optimism — that "whatever is, is right" — that Smith engages with critically in his account of the human tendency to confound the useful with the good

memes.label: An Inquiry into the Original of Our Ideas of Beauty and Virtue
memes.description: Francis Hutcheson's major work (1725) on moral philosophy, developing the theory of the moral sense and the doctrine that virtue consists in benevolence — the foundation Smith built upon and departed from

memes.label: Approbation
memes.description: The sentiment of moral approval — the felt recognition that a passion or action is proper, arising from the correspondence between the spectator's sympathetic feelings and the agent's original passion

memes.label: Aristotle
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (384–322 BCE), student of Plato — Smith draws on Aristotle's virtue ethics, particularly the doctrine of the mean, while transforming it through the mechanism of sympathy and the standard of the impartial spectator

memes.label: Benevolence
memes.description: The virtue of wishing and doing good to others — for Smith, the ornament that adorns society but is less essential than justice for its survival

memes.label: Bernard Mandeville
memes.description: Dutch-English philosopher (1670–1733) whose cynical reduction of all virtue to disguised self-interest in The Fable of the Bees provoked Smith to develop his more nuanced account of human moral psychology
aliases: author of the Fable of the Bees

memes.label: Cicero
memes.description: Roman statesman and philosopher (106–43 BCE) whose works on duty and the Stoic concept of decorum, particularly De Officiis, provided Smith with key concepts for his theory of propriety

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The internalized impartial spectator — the faculty by which we judge our own conduct, operating as "the man within the breast" who praises or condemns us independently of what others actually think

memes.label: David Hume
memes.description: Scottish philosopher and historian (1711–1776), Smith's closest friend — though rarely named in TMS, Hume's theory of sympathy as the foundation of moral judgment profoundly influenced Smith's own account

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The binding force of moral rules — for Smith, the product of generalizing sympathetic judgments into rules that conscience enforces, giving moral life the character of law without requiring a divine lawgiver

memes.label: Epictetus
memes.description: Stoic philosopher (c. 50–135 CE), a freed slave whose teachings on self-command and the distinction between what is and is not in our power profoundly influenced Smith's account of the "awful and respectable" virtues

memes.label: Epicurus
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (341–270 BCE) whose doctrine that pleasure is the sole good and that all virtue reduces to prudence represents, for Smith, the most sophisticated version of the theory that virtue consists in self-interest

memes.label: Francis Hutcheson
memes.description: Scottish Enlightenment philosopher (1694–1746), Smith's teacher at Glasgow, whose theory of the moral sense and emphasis on benevolence deeply influenced — and was critiqued by — The Theory of Moral Sentiments

memes.label: Impartial Spectator
memes.description: Smith's most original philosophical contribution — an imagined impartial observer whose sympathetic responses provide the standard by which we judge our own conduct, internalized as conscience

memes.label: Isaac Newton
memes.description: English natural philosopher (1642–1727) whose scientific method — explaining diverse phenomena through a few simple principles — provided Smith with a methodological model for his own project of explaining moral phenomena through the single principle of sympathy

memes.label: Jonathan Swift
memes.description: Anglo-Irish satirist (1667–1745) whose unflinching depiction of human folly and vice — particularly in Gulliver's Travels — represents the misanthropic extreme against which Smith's humane moral psychology defines itself

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The negative virtue that prohibits harming others — the foundation of social order, backed by resentment and punishment, which Smith treats as more essential to society than benevolence

memes.label: Marcus Aurelius
memes.description: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher (121–180 CE) whose Meditations represent the practical summit of Stoic ethics — Smith draws on Marcus Aurelius's account of self-command and the discipline of judgment

memes.label: Meditations
memes.description: Marcus Aurelius's private philosophical notebook (c. 170–180 CE) — the practical culmination of Stoic ethics, emphasizing self-discipline, the dichotomy of control, and cosmopolitan benevolence

memes.label: Merit and Demerit
memes.description: The second dimension of moral judgment in Smith's system — the sense that an action deserves reward or punishment, based on the sympathetic response to the gratitude or resentment it naturally excites

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher (c. 428–348 BCE), founder of the Academy — Smith engages with Plato's theory of the tripartite soul and his account of virtue as harmony, positioning his own theory against the Platonic rationalist tradition

memes.label: Propriety
memes.description: The central standard of moral judgment in Smith's theory — an action or passion is proper when it is proportionate to its cause, as measured by whether an impartial spectator can sympathize with it

memes.label: Prudence
memes.description: The virtue of wisely pursuing one's own long-term welfare — for Smith, a respectable but merely "amiable" virtue that lacks the demanding quality of full moral virtue

memes.label: Resentment
memes.description: The unsocial passion that arises from perceived injury — for Smith, the natural guardian of justice, whose propriety is judged by the sympathetic response of the impartial spectator

memes.label: Self-Command
memes.description: The virtue of moderating one's passions to the pitch at which the impartial spectator can sympathize — for Smith, the foundation of human dignity and the complement to sensibility

memes.label: Self-Love
memes.description: The natural concern for one's own welfare — for Smith, a legitimate and powerful human motive that must be regulated by the impartial spectator, not eliminated, and distinguished from mere selfishness

memes.label: Sympathy
memes.description: The central mechanism of moral judgment in Smith's theory — our natural capacity to enter into the feelings of others through imagination, forming the foundation of all moral sentiments

memes.label: The Fable of the Bees
memes.description: Bernard Mandeville's scandalous work (1714) arguing that private vices produce public benefits — the provocation that spurred Smith, Hutcheson, and an entire generation of moral philosophers to defend the reality of virtue
aliases: The Fable of the Bees: or

memes.label: The Theory of Moral Sentiments
memes.description: Adam Smith's first major work (1759), developing a comprehensive theory of moral psychology grounded in the single principle of sympathy — the foundation of this wiki

memes.label: The Wealth of Nations
memes.description: Adam Smith's magnum opus (1776) on political economy — though distinct in subject from TMS, it rests on the same moral psychology and theory of human nature
aliases: An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: English philosopher (1588–1679) whose theory that human nature is fundamentally selfish and that morality arises from social contract represents the egoist position that Smith's entire moral psychology is designed to refute

memes.label: Utility
memes.description: The quality of being conducive to some end — for Smith, a secondary source of moral approbation that reinforces but does not ground our moral judgments, contrary to Hume's theory

memes.label: Vanity
memes.description: The desire for the admiration and attention of others — for Smith, a powerful but morally ambiguous social passion that drives much of human industry and ambition

memes.label: Voltaire
memes.description: French Enlightenment philosopher (1694–1778) — Smith engages with Voltaire's critique of religious intolerance and Leibnizian optimism, finding in Voltaire's satire a powerful but incomplete moral vision

memes.label: Zeno of Citium
memes.description: Founder of Stoicism (c. 334–262 BCE), whose doctrine that virtue consists in living according to nature and that the passions are errors of judgment established the framework that Smith both draws upon and criticizes
